title: The Pitch

jokes.text: He's got the three-cornered hat, knickers...and the Def Leppard shirt.
jokes.setup: Supposedly authentic blacksmith at Colonial Williamsburg
jokes.punchline: Wearing anachronistic Def Leppard shirt with period costume
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational comedy about historical reenactment authenticity. The specific band choice and visual contrast work well.

jokes.text: So it's the whole community that's been grounded.
jokes.setup: Amish people have no cars, TV, phones - like a kid without privileges
jokes.punchline: Reframing entire Amish lifestyle as collective punishment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant recontextualization that's both absurd and logically sound from a child's perspective. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: Don't come down till you make some noodles and raise a barn.
jokes.setup: Parent threatening to punish child by making them Amish
jokes.punchline: Escalating to specific Amish activities as punishment requirements
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Amish punishment concept with specific activities that sound appropriately arduous.

jokes.text: What did they do for toilet paper in the Civil War?
jokes.setup: Historical battlefield tour setting
jokes.punchline: George's immediate fixation on mundane bathroom logistics
jokes.explanation: Perfect George character moment - obsessing over practical details while missing bigger picture. Sets up his toilet paper fixation.

jokes.text: Everything with you comes down to toilet paper...That's always the first question with you.
jokes.setup: George asking about Civil War toilet paper
jokes.punchline: Jerry pointing out George's consistent toilet paper obsession
jokes.explanation: Good character observation but fairly straightforward. Establishes George's quirky priorities.

jokes.text: They should have a toilet-paper museum. Would you like that?
jokes.setup: Discussion of historical toilet paper record
jokes.punchline: George's earnest enthusiasm for a toilet paper museum
jokes.explanation: Absurd but believable George would genuinely want this. The earnest 'would you like that?' sells it.

jokes.text: The development of the perforation. The first six-pack.
jokes.setup: Imagining toilet paper museum exhibits
jokes.punchline: Specific mundane 'innovations' presented as museum-worthy achievements
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with specific details. 'First six-pack' is particularly funny as a historical milestone.

jokes.text: I'm unbelievable at spelling last names. Give give me a last name.
jokes.setup: NBC executive meeting Jerry, normal introduction
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely specific boast about spelling ability, demanding demonstration
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific and awkward character quirk. The insistence on demonstration makes it cringier.

jokes.text: George. All right. All right.
jokes.setup: Stu demanding a last name to spell
jokes.punchline: Jerry offering George's first name instead, George's resigned acceptance
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective awkward moment. George's deflated acceptance sells the absurdity.

jokes.text: You coach a gymnastics team in high school, and you're married.
jokes.setup: Jerry needs TV show ideas
jokes.punchline: George's oddly specific, random concept with no connection to Jerry
jokes.explanation: Captures George's tendency to generate random, unsuitable ideas with complete confidence.

jokes.text: Why should I care if my son's into gymnastics? - You're the teacher. It's only natural. - But gymnastics is not for everybody.
jokes.setup: George pitching his gymnastics show idea
jokes.punchline: Jerry's logical objections exposing the concept's fundamental flaws
jokes.explanation: Good dynamic showing Jerry's practical skepticism vs George's illogical enthusiasm.

jokes.text: What person who runs an antique store gets involved in people's lives?
jokes.setup: George suggests Jerry runs antique store and gets involved in customers' lives
jokes.punchline: Jerry's logical objection about antique dealers' typical involvement level
jokes.explanation: Sharp logical takedown of a generic TV premise. Shows Jerry's analytical mind.

jokes.text: I could see if I was a pharmacist. Because a pharmacist knows what's wrong with everybody.
jokes.setup: Rejecting antique store premise
jokes.punchline: Logical alternative profession that would actually justify getting involved
jokes.explanation: Smart observation about professional relationships. Shows Jerry's logical thinking process.

jokes.text: Who wouldn't tune in to see a woman with a mustache?
jokes.setup: George pitching circus manager show
jokes.punchline: George's misguided confidence about exploiting physical differences for entertainment
jokes.explanation: Perfect George - completely missing social sensitivity while being enthusiastic about bad ideas.

jokes.text: A guy who's just a head.
jokes.setup: George listing circus 'freaks' for his show idea
jokes.punchline: Impossibly absurd 'person' that couldn't actually exist
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation that shows George's disconnect from reality. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: Look, the show isn't about the circus. It's about watching freaks.
jokes.setup: Jerry objecting to circus show idea
jokes.punchline: George's brutally honest admission about the show's actual appeal
jokes.explanation: George's unfiltered honesty about exploitative entertainment. Character-perfect lack of self-awareness.

jokes.text: People, they wanna watch freaks. This is a 'can't miss.'
jokes.setup: George defending his circus freak show idea
jokes.punchline: Complete confidence in obviously problematic concept
jokes.explanation: George's delusional confidence in terrible ideas is perfectly in character. The enthusiasm makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I had a girlfriend. And she was pretty wild. - I don't remember you with a girl. - Nevertheless.
jokes.setup: Kramer claiming to have had a wild girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Jerry's skepticism met with Kramer's unfazed 'Nevertheless'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer moment. 'Nevertheless' as a complete dismissal of obvious contradiction is brilliant deadpan.

jokes.text: Hey, you know you're getting gypped over here.
jokes.setup: George warns Jerry not to interfere in Kramer's bad trade
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately ignoring the advice and interfering anyway
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jerry can't help but interfere despite agreeing not to.

jokes.text: Are you reneging out of the deal? Are you reneging? - That's a renege. - Stop saying 'reneging.'
jokes.setup: Newman using 'reneging' repeatedly
jokes.punchline: Kramer's annoyance at the word repetition
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay escalation. The repetition of 'reneging' creates mild irritation comedy.

jokes.text: Does that thing work? - No.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks about the radar detector Kramer just traded for
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual admission it doesn't work
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline with maximum economy. Kramer's casual delivery of devastating information.

jokes.text: Salsa is now the number-one condiment in America. Do you know why? Because people like to say 'salsa.'
jokes.setup: George asking why there's no salsa on the table
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous theory that popularity is based on pronunciation enjoyment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd logic that sounds almost plausible. The theory is completely George - overthinking simple things.

jokes.text: Do you have any salsa? We need more salsa. Where is the salsa? No salsa?
jokes.setup: George's theory about people liking to say 'salsa'
jokes.punchline: George immediately demonstrating by repeatedly saying 'salsa' with increasing urgency
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that proves his own point through demonstration. Physical comedy of increasingly desperate salsa requests.

jokes.text: You know, it must be impossible for a Spanish person to order seltzer and not get salsa.
jokes.setup: Discussion about salsa pronunciation
jokes.punchline: Logical extension about pronunciation confusion in Spanish
jokes.explanation: Smart observational comedy building on the salsa premise. The pronunciation confusion is believable and funny.

jokes.text: I wanted seltzer, not salsa. Don't you know the difference between them? You have the seltzer after the salsa.
jokes.setup: Jerry imagining Spanish person's confusion
jokes.punchline: Escalating frustration with logical but absurd meal sequence explanation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the confusion with Jerry's characteristic over-explanation.

jokes.text: This should be the show. This is the show. - What? - This. Just talking.
jokes.setup: Jerry realizing their current conversation could be the show
jokes.punchline: The meta-revelation that mundane conversation is entertainment
jokes.explanation: Revolutionary meta-comedy moment. The show literally discovering its own premise is brilliant and became television history.

jokes.text: It's about nothing. - No story? - No, forget the story. - You gotta have a story. - Who says you gotta have a story?
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining the show concept
jokes.punchline: Fundamental challenge to basic television storytelling conventions
jokes.explanation: Iconic dialogue that became the show's defining philosophy. Genuinely revolutionary concept delivered with perfect simplicity.

jokes.text: Remember when we were waiting for that table in that Chinese restaurant? That could be a TV show.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining the 'nothing' concept
jokes.punchline: Reference to actual mundane experience as potential episode
jokes.explanation: Meta-reference to what would become a famous Seinfeld episode. Shows how mundane experiences can be compelling.

jokes.text: People are always saying to me, 'You know, you're quite a character.'
jokes.setup: George explaining why he should be a character on the show
jokes.punchline: George's literal interpretation of a common expression
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - completely missing the figurative meaning. Shows his literal-minded self-importance.

jokes.text: We? Since when are you a writer? - What writer? We're talking about a sitcom.
jokes.setup: Jerry questioning George's sudden writing partnership
jokes.punchline: George's dismissive attitude toward sitcom writing as requiring skill
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing George's arrogance and dismissiveness. Also self-deprecating about sitcom writing.

jokes.text: I don't like it. - What don't you like? - I don't like the idea of a character based on me.
jokes.setup: Jerry wants to base a character on Kramer
jokes.punchline: Kramer objecting to being portrayed on TV despite his eccentric behavior
jokes.explanation: Ironic that the most eccentric character would object to TV portrayal. Good character-based comedy.

jokes.text: I get to play Kramer. - You can't play Kramer. - I am Kramer. - But you can't act.
jokes.setup: Kramer demanding to play himself
jokes.punchline: Jerry's brutal honesty about Kramer's lack of acting ability
jokes.explanation: Good logical progression and Jerry's characteristic bluntness. The irony that he can't play himself.

jokes.text: Okay, fine, we'll use Newman.
jokes.setup: Kramer can't play himself due to acting inability
jokes.punchline: Jerry's casual suggestion to use Newman instead
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jerry's practical problem-solving and casual dismissiveness.

jokes.text: Because the radar detector, as I understand it, detects radar! With a series of beeps and flashing lights.
jokes.setup: Newman explaining his radar detector didn't work
jokes.punchline: Newman's sarcastic over-explanation of how radar detectors obviously should work
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic delivery showing Newman's indignation. The over-explanation adds to the humor.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, yeah. You better think again, MoJumbo.
jokes.setup: Newman confronting Kramer about the defective radar detector
jokes.punchline: Newman's bizarre insult combining 'mojo' and 'jumbo'
jokes.explanation: Weird Newman insult that's memorable for its strangeness. 'MoJumbo' is oddly threatening.

jokes.text: Karma Kramer?
jokes.setup: Newman threatening karma will get Kramer back
jokes.punchline: Accidental rhyme creating 'Karma Kramer' as a name
jokes.explanation: Accidental clever wordplay that sounds like a superhero name. The rhythm makes it memorable.

jokes.text: Salsa, seltzer. Do you have any salsa? No, not seltzer, salsa.
jokes.setup: Jerry's earlier observation about Spanish pronunciation confusion
jokes.punchline: Jerry demonstrating the confusion himself at the restaurant
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier joke, showing the practical application of Jerry's theory.

jokes.text: They're men with jobs, Jerry! They wear suits and ties. They're married. They have secretaries!
jokes.setup: George panicking about meeting TV executives
jokes.punchline: George's escalating list of adult responsibilities that intimidate him
jokes.explanation: Perfect George panic. The escalation from jobs to secretaries shows his childlike fear of adult success.

jokes.text: That's pretty good.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying executives will give them water inside
jokes.punchline: George's pathetic gratitude for basic hospitality
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - finding comfort in the most basic things when panicking.

jokes.text: You're under no obligation to shake my hand.
jokes.setup: Normal greeting situation with Jerry
jokes.punchline: Davola's bizarre formal disclaimer about handshaking
jokes.explanation: Perfectly creepy Davola line. The formal language makes it more unsettling and funny.

jokes.text: You do the karate, right? - Yeah, you wanna hit me?
jokes.setup: Jerry making awkward small talk about Davola's karate
jokes.punchline: Davola's immediate offer of violence as casual conversation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from awkward small talk to violence offer. Davola's casual delivery is perfect.

jokes.text: He's doing something. I don't know. It's nothing. He's not doing anything.
jokes.setup: Jerry trying to cover up about Kramer's party
jokes.punchline: Jerry's increasingly contradictory explanations making it worse
jokes.explanation: Good panic response showing Jerry making things worse by over-explaining.

jokes.text: Give my best to Hinckley.
jokes.setup: Davola ending conversation with Jerry
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to would-be presidential assassin John Hinckley
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark Davola line that reveals his instability through casual reference to famous assassin.

jokes.text: Who are they? - They're nobody. - What about me? - What about you? - Why them? Why not me? - Why not you? - I'm just as good as them. - Better. - You really think so? - No.
jokes.setup: George's escalating panic and self-doubt about the executives
jokes.punchline: Jerry's brutal honesty after building up George's confidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic rhythm building to Jerry's devastating honesty. The escalation and punchline are expertly timed.

jokes.text: La Cocina. Actually, it was off-off Broadway.
jokes.setup: George claiming to have written an off-Broadway show
jokes.punchline: Immediate downgrade to even more obscure 'off-off Broadway'
jokes.explanation: Good George deflation - immediately undermining his own claim. Shows his insecurity and dishonesty.

jokes.text: No, no, he mimed it. That's what was so funny about it.
jokes.setup: Question about whether chef actually cooked on stage
jokes.punchline: George's delusional belief that miming cooking is hilarious
jokes.explanation: Perfect George - completely missing what would actually be funny. His confidence in bad ideas is character-perfect.

jokes.text: I think I can sum up the show for you with one word: Nothing.
jokes.setup: George offering to explain their show concept
jokes.punchline: The dramatic buildup to the anti-climactic word 'nothing'
jokes.explanation: Iconic moment with perfect dramatic structure. The buildup makes 'nothing' devastatingly funny.

jokes.text: Well, maybe in philosophy. But even nothing is something.
jokes.setup: George saying the show is about nothing
jokes.punchline: Philosophical objection to the concept of nothingness
jokes.explanation: Smart intellectual response that shows the executive trying to engage with the concept seriously.

jokes.text: D-A-L-R-l-M-P-E-L. Not even close.
jokes.setup: Secretary announces Mr. Dalrymple, callback to Stu's spelling obsession
jokes.punchline: Stu's admission he was completely wrong about the spelling
jokes.explanation: Good callback that deflates Stu's earlier confident boast. The honesty of 'not even close' is funny.

jokes.text: You read? - Well, I don't know about the reading. We didn't discuss the reading.
jokes.setup: Jerry mentioning reading as a life activity for the show
jokes.punchline: Executive's confusion and Jerry's retreat from this 'controversial' concept
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist moment where reading becomes controversial TV content. Shows network thinking.

jokes.text: Well, why am I watching it? - Because it's on TV. - Not yet.
jokes.setup: Executive asking why anyone would watch a show where nothing happens
jokes.punchline: George's circular logic that people watch because it's on TV, Jerry's reality check
jokes.explanation: Brilliant circular logic joke with perfect timing on Jerry's reality check. Meta-commentary on TV viewing habits.

jokes.text: I, for one, am not going to compromise my artistic integrity.
jokes.setup: George responding to network suggestions
jokes.punchline: George claiming artistic integrity despite having no artistic background
jokes.explanation: Perfect George delusion. The pompous language from someone with no credentials is beautifully absurd.

jokes.text: How about this? I manage a circus.
jokes.setup: George's artistic integrity stance failing
jokes.punchline: Immediate retreat to his earlier bad circus idea
jokes.explanation: Perfect George collapse from high principles to desperate compromise. The speed of the reversal is hilarious.

jokes.text: You're not artistic, and you have no integrity.
jokes.setup: George claiming artistic integrity
jokes.punchline: Jerry's brutal systematic destruction of George's claim
jokes.explanation: Devastating character assassination delivered with Jerry's characteristic precision. Double-barreled insult structure.

jokes.text: A team of psychiatrists, working round the clock, thinking about you, having conferences. Observing you, like the way they did with the Elephant Man.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying George needs psychiatric help
jokes.punchline: Escalating to George needing treatment like a medical curiosity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation comparing George to the Elephant Man. The clinical language makes it funnier and more brutal.

jokes.text: I thought the woman was kind of cute.
jokes.setup: Jerry's devastating psychological analysis of George
jokes.punchline: George's complete non-sequitur about finding Susan attractive
jokes.explanation: Perfect George non-sequitur showing his ability to ignore criticism and focus on women. Timing is perfect.

jokes.text: We had good eye contact through the whole meeting.
jokes.setup: George explaining why Susan might like him
jokes.punchline: George's delusional interpretation of professional eye contact
jokes.explanation: Perfect George delusion about romantic signals. His confidence in misreading situations is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, hello. Oh, hello. - Hello.
jokes.setup: Kramer answering Jerry's phone
jokes.punchline: Awkward repeated greetings suggesting Kramer's confusion or social awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Simple Kramer awkwardness moment. The repetition suggests his social confusion.

jokes.text: Why don't you give me your home number, and I'll call you later? - I'm sorry. We're not allowed to do that. - I guess you don't want people calling you at home. - No. - Well, now you know how I feel.
jokes.setup: Telemarketer calling Kramer, standard sales call
jokes.punchline: Kramer turning the tables by requesting telemarketer's home number
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal joke that became a cultural reference. Perfect logic and structure, genuinely clever observation.

jokes.text: September third. - The third? - The third?
jokes.setup: Jerry asking about milk expiration date
jokes.punchline: Escalating horror at realizing the milk is severely expired
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure building to the vomiting payoff. The repetition creates anticipation.

jokes.text: Vomiting is not a deal-breaker. If Hitler had vomited on Chamberlain, he still would have given him Czechoslovakia.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying vomiting on Susan killed their deal
jokes.punchline: George's absurd historical analogy about vomiting and appeasement
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant absurd analogy. Comparing vomiting to Nazi appeasement is genuinely original and memorable.

jokes.text: You could hold his head in the toilet, he'd still give you half of Europe.
jokes.setup: George's Chamberlain appeasement analogy
jokes.punchline: Escalating the humiliation and the territorial concessions
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the Hitler/Chamberlain bit. The specific humiliation and territorial exchange is perfect.

jokes.text: That's what I think of your party.
jokes.setup: Davola attacking Kramer with karate kick
jokes.punchline: Davola's formal explanation for the violence
jokes.explanation: Good Davola violence with his characteristic formal speech pattern. The contrast between violence and politeness.

jokes.text: When you vomit on somebody, it is a social faux pas from which there is really no recovery.
jokes.setup: Jerry doing stand-up about vomiting social situations
jokes.punchline: Formal language describing the ultimate social disaster
jokes.explanation: Good observational comedy with Jerry's characteristic formal analysis of social situations.

jokes.text: There's no vomit sympathy-card section of the store, you know.
jokes.setup: Jerry discussing lack of social recovery from vomiting
jokes.punchline: Specific observation about greeting card industry gap
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational detail about social conventions. The specificity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: 'Next time, lunch is on me.'
jokes.setup: Jerry imagining inappropriate vomit sympathy cards
jokes.punchline: Double entendre about literal and figurative meaning of 'lunch is on me'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay with perfect double meaning. The economy and cleverness are exceptional.

jokes.text: What is this? Why do I got one pant leg on for?
jokes.setup: Kramer discovering he's only wearing one pant leg
jokes.punchline: Kramer's confused reaction to his own bizarre condition
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup. Kramer's confusion at his own condition is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: You only shaved the right side of your face.
jokes.setup: Kramer's one pant leg situation
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Kramer has only half-shaved his face too
jokes.explanation: Good escalation revealing Kramer's systematic half-completion of grooming tasks.

jokes.text: Yo-Yo Ma!
jokes.setup: Discussion of Kramer's brain injury symptoms
jokes.punchline: Kramer randomly shouting cellist's name
jokes.explanation: Random absurdist moment that becomes a running gag. The unexpectedness and economy make it memorable.

jokes.text: Can you get vomit out of suede?
jokes.setup: Discussion of Susan's dry cleaning bill
jokes.punchline: Practical question about fabric care after vomiting
jokes.explanation: Good practical follow-up question that extends the vomiting comedy. Jerry's characteristic attention to details.

jokes.text: They wanna buy it! They wanna buy it! What did I tell you?
jokes.setup: NBC calling back about the show
jokes.punchline: George's triumphant vindication and 'I told you so' attitude
jokes.explanation: Perfect George victory dance moment. His immediate claim of prescience is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Ted Danson makes $800,000 an episode. - Would you stop with the Ted Danson?
jokes.setup: George obsessing over money expectations
jokes.punchline: George's completely unrealistic comparison to established star
jokes.explanation: Good George delusion about his worth. Jerry's exasperation shows this is an ongoing obsession.

jokes.text: Yeah, don't worry. He can afford it.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Ted Danson wearing a hairpiece
jokes.punchline: George's matter-of-fact acceptance that wealth justifies vanity
jokes.explanation: George's practical view of wealth and appearance. Shows his materialism and envy.

jokes.text: I sleep in my underwear and a T-shirt. If it gets too hot, I just take the T-shirt off.
jokes.setup: Leo discussing pajamas he can't wear
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily detailed description of his sleeping arrangements
jokes.explanation: Perfect Uncle Leo oversharing moment. The unnecessary detail about temperature control is characteristic.

jokes.text: I know plenty of people in Hollywood too.
jokes.setup: Jerry dismissing Leo to get to NBC meeting
jokes.punchline: Leo's defensive claim about Hollywood connections
jokes.explanation: Good Uncle Leo moment showing his competitiveness and exaggeration tendencies.

jokes.text: There are many things that I think you can point to as proof that the humans are not smart. But my personal favorite would have to be that we had to invent the helmet.
jokes.setup: Jerry doing stand-up about human intelligence
jokes.punchline: Helmet invention as evidence of human stupidity rather than ingenuity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational reversal. Taking protective gear as evidence of stupidity is genuinely clever perspective shift.

jokes.text: The idea behind the helmet law is to preserve a brain whose judgment is so poor, it does not even try to stop the cracking of the head it's in.
jokes.setup: Jerry's helmet invention observation
jokes.punchline: Logical extension about helmet laws protecting poor judgment
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the helmet premise with sophisticated logic. The circular reasoning is brilliantly constructed.

jokes.text: Go. Go ahead. Go to your fancy fire. If that's what you have to do.
jokes.setup: Jerry imagining relative's guilt trip even during emergency
jokes.punchline: Uncle Leo's sarcastic dismissal of life-saving fire department work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Uncle Leo impression with 'fancy fire' as dismissive term for emergency. Character integration is flawless.

jokes.text: I never had an air conditioner.
jokes.setup: Newman needs reason for fake suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: Completely inadequate reason for wanting to die
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist reasoning for suicide. The inadequacy of the motivation is perfectly Newman.

jokes.text: I was never able to become a banker.
jokes.setup: Newman trying different suicide motivation
jokes.punchline: Callback to earlier Kramer banking obsession
jokes.explanation: Good callback tying plots together. The banking obsession returning is structurally sound.

jokes.text: Story is the foundation of all entertainment. You must have a good story. Otherwise, it's just masturbation.
jokes.setup: NBC executive explaining storytelling importance
jokes.punchline: Crude but vivid analogy for self-indulgent entertainment
jokes.explanation: Sharp, crude but effective analogy about entertainment value. The bluntness from an executive is funny.

jokes.text: This show will be like a heart attack. Just a huge, massive coronary.
jokes.setup: Executive comparing shows to EKG patterns
jokes.punchline: Their show as medical emergency rather than entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good dark metaphor extending the EKG analogy. The medical emergency comparison is apt and funny.

jokes.text: I said... I said, 'Get out of here. No story? Is that what I said?'
jokes.setup: George explaining his reaction to hearing about their 'no story' concept
jokes.punchline: George's fake outrage at his own idea to seem more credible
jokes.explanation: Perfect George lying and backtracking. His fake shock at his own idea is character-perfect deception.

jokes.text: Hey, Yo-Yo Ma.
jokes.setup: Courtroom testimony about Newman's suicide prevention call
jokes.punchline: Kramer's brain injury symptom appearing in formal setting
jokes.explanation: Good callback placing the running gag in formal courtroom setting for maximum disruption.

jokes.text: See! Told you! I told you!
jokes.setup: Newman pointing out Kramer in court as his suicidal friend
jokes.punchline: Kramer's enthusiastic validation of Newman's lie
jokes.explanation: Good Kramer moment where he enthusiastically validates a lie he doesn't understand.

jokes.text: I can't believe she took the money.
jokes.setup: Jerry paying Susan for dry cleaning
jokes.punchline: Jerry's surprise that Susan actually accepted payment
jokes.explanation: Good observation about social expectations vs reality. Jerry expected polite refusal.

jokes.text: Ted Danson makes $800,000 an episode. - Would you stop with the Ted Danson? - Well, he does. - You're nuts.
jokes.setup: George still obsessing over Ted Danson's salary
jokes.punchline: Jerry's complete exasperation with the comparison
jokes.explanation: Good running gag showing George's persistent delusions. Jerry's exasperation escalates appropriately.

jokes.text: You know, a muffin can be very filling.
jokes.setup: Cop ordering full meal instead of promised muffin
jokes.punchline: Jerry's passive-aggressive comment about food choices
jokes.explanation: Good passive-aggressive Jerry moment. The understatement masks his annoyance effectively.

jokes.text: What do you mean, 'in my own words'? Whose words are they gonna be?
jokes.setup: Court asking Kramer to testify 'in his own words'
jokes.punchline: Kramer's literal interpretation of common legal phrase
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer literal-mindedness. His confusion at standard legal language is character-perfect.

jokes.text: 'Sonny boy, take a good look at him. That's gonna be you someday.'
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining his father's banking inspiration
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic parental guidance about banking career
jokes.explanation: Good backstory explaining the banking obsession. The fatherly guidance tone is appropriately dramatic.

jokes.text: The punishment should be, instead of traffic school or traffic court, just traffic. They sentence you to 100 hours of traffic.
jokes.setup: Jerry discussing traffic school ineffectiveness
jokes.punchline: Poetic justice concept of traffic as punishment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant concept of poetic justice punishment. The logic is sound and the idea is genuinely creative.

jokes.text: Five people drive all around you at five miles an hour wherever you go.
jokes.setup: Jerry's traffic punishment concept
jokes.punchline: Specific implementation of personal traffic hell
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with specific details. The personal traffic hell concept is vividly realized.

tmdb_overview: A show about "nothing" is George's pitch to network execs interested in a series for Jerry.
